subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor's Degree, completed in 2022.
Majored in Journalism with a minor in Data Science.
Completed coursework in data analysis, investigative reporting, and statistics.
Participated in a college data journalism workshop before graduation.
Attended several online training sessions on R and Python basics.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title is Data Reporter.
Role combines investigative journalism with data analysis.
Responsible for finding, cleaning, analyzing, and visualizing data for news stories.
Regularly interviews sources and conducts research to complement data findings.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the media and journalism industry.
Specializes in the data journalism segment of news reporting.
Focuses on uncovering stories through public and proprietary datasets.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Works for a mid-sized digital news organization with approximately 80-120 employees.
The newsroom environment is collaborative and deadline-driven.
Data journalists make up a small, specialized team within the newsroom.
Work is hybrid, with both remote and in-office collaboration.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Company operates as a digital-first news outlet.
Covers local and national news with a focus on data-driven stories.
Serves a general public readership as well as industry professionals.
Revenue comes from subscriptions, advertising, and grants for investigative journalism.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a data reporter.
Started current position shortly after college graduation.
Previously completed internships in journalism and data analysis.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds an entry-level to early-career position.
Has not yet taken on managerial or editorial oversight responsibilities.
Works under the guidance of senior reporters and editors.
Regularly collaborates with peers and occasionally mentors interns.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Sources, cleans, and analyzes datasets for potential news stories.
Conducts interviews with experts and sources to supplement data findings.
Writes and edits articles based on data analysis and reporting.
Creates charts and visualizations using Datawrapper or Flourish.
Collaborates with editors to refine story angles and presentation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends about 40% of time on data cleaning and analysis.
Approximately 25% of time is dedicated to reporting and interviewing.
Roughly 20% of workweek is spent writing articles and editing copy.
Devotes 10% to data visualization tasks.
The remaining 5% covers administrative work and meetings.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has autonomy in selecting datasets and initial story ideas.
Major editorial decisions are made in collaboration with senior editors.
Can independently decide on analytical methods and visualization styles.
Final publishing decisions require editorial approval.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Data cleaning and wrangling in R feels straightforward.
Using Google Sheets to perform basic analyses is intuitive.
Conducting source interviews comes naturally due to journalism training.
Drafting initial story outlines is often a smooth process.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Scraping data from poorly structured websites can be difficult.
Analyzing large or messy datasets with multiple inconsistencies presents challenges.
Creating custom, interactive visualizations requires advanced coding skills.
Balancing data accuracy with tight publishing deadlines adds complexity.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Identifying and removing duplicate rows from datasets is repetitive.
Standardizing date and location formats could be automated.
Routine data validation checks are time-consuming and repetitive.
Generating basic summary statistics is well-suited to automation.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Finding unexpected patterns in data is highly engaging.
Interviewing sources and uncovering new perspectives is enjoyable.
Manual data entry or repetitive cleaning feels tedious.
Formatting charts for publication can become monotonous over time.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Dealing with incomplete or inconsistent public datasets delays analysis.
Switching between multiple tools (R, Sheets, Datawrapper) creates friction.
Waiting for data requests or FOIA responses can stall reporting.
Limited IT support sometimes hinders troubleshooting technical issues.

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Tasks that align with strongest skills, like data analysis in R, are both enjoyable and productive.
Learning new visualization techniques is enjoyable but may initially reduce productivity due to a learning curve.
Repetitive tasks lower engagement and can lead to reduced productivity.
Enjoyment and confidence in a task often correlate with higher output quality.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Has a positive, proactive attitude towards exploring new tech.
Frequently seeks out new software tools to improve workflow.
Views tech learning as essential for staying competitive in journalism.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new data analysis and visualization tools.
Comfortable experimenting with emerging technologies, including AI tools.
Relies on online tutorials and peer recommendations to learn new software.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to spend personal time on tech upskilling, especially for tools relevant to data journalism.
Attends webinars and workshops when available.
Prefers hands-on, project-based learning over formal coursework.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the ability to tell more impactful stories with advanced tools.
Heavy workload can sometimes limit time available for learning.
Relevance to current projects is a major factor in deciding what to learn.
Occasional lack of structured support from company can be a barrier.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often suggest new tools, influencing willingness to try them.
Management encourages experimentation with innovative technologies.
Collaborative environment supports sharing tech tips and best practices.
Occasionally, team adoption rates affect whether a tool is integrated into workflow.

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses R for most data cleaning and analysis tasks.
Employs Google Sheets for quick data exploration and collaboration.
Utilizes Datawrapper for creating charts and graphs for publication.
Occasionally leverages Python for specific data tasks.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on Google Docs for drafting articles and collaborative editing.
Uses Slack for team communication and updates.
Leverages email for official communication and data requests.
Uses Datawrapper and Flourish for non-AI data visualization.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Familiar with Excel but prefers Google Sheets for collaboration.
Has working knowledge of Python and Jupyter Notebooks.
Aware of Tableau, but does not use it regularly in current workflow.
Understands basics of SQL for querying structured data.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Exporting data between R and Google Sheets sometimes requires manual formatting.
Copy-pasting charts from Datawrapper to CMS can lead to formatting errors.
Some visualization tools do not natively support R data frames.
Occasional incompatibility between data formats slows workflow.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands key terms such as language models, prompt engineering, and supervised learning.
Knows the basics of how large language models (LLMs) work.
Familiar with machine learning concepts like training data, overfitting, and model evaluation.
Reads industry news to stay updated on AI advancements in journalism.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT and other LLMs regularly for troubleshooting code.
Employs Google Bard for brainstorming and outlining stories.
Occasionally uses Github Copilot to assist with R or Python scripting.
Tests out emerging AI tools for data analysis as part of ongoing workflow improvements.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Engages with LLMs weekly to debug code and resolve data processing errors.
Uses AI to suggest steps or methods for complex data analysis.
Leverages AI for brainstorming headlines and article structure.
Occasionally uses AI to summarize lengthy documents or datasets.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully resolved a recurring R error by consulting ChatGPT, saving hours of troubleshooting.
Noticed that AI sometimes suggests incorrect or outdated code, requiring manual verification.
Used AI to generate initial data visualizations, but found results too generic for publication.
Learned the importance of clear prompts to get accurate AI outputs.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal company-wide AI training program currently in place.
Peers often share AI tips and prompt examples informally via Slack.
Relies on self-directed learning and online resources for AI skill development.
Occasionally attends external webinars on AI in journalism.

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI when solving complex coding problems.
Uses independent queries for quick fact-checking or idea generation.
Switches between modes depending on task complexity and urgency.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as an assistant for routine code and writing support.
Relies on AI as an advisor when brainstorming data analysis approaches.
Treats AI as a collaborative co-worker during creative or technical blocks.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to experimenting with AI-driven workflows that save time.
Evaluates new AI tools based on their ability to improve story accuracy and speed.
Actively seeks out feedback from peers on effective AI use cases.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces (like ChatGPT) for brainstorming and troubleshooting.
Uses command-based interfaces for scripting and direct code execution.
Finds conversational AI more approachable for exploring new ideas.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Data analysis requires understanding of journalistic ethics and story context.
Interviewing sources and interpreting data trends involve tacit knowledge.
Story framing and narrative construction depend heavily on domain expertise.
Code troubleshooting is more technical, with less reliance on domain-specific nuance.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Moderate trust in AI for code suggestions and idea generation.
Always manually reviews AI-generated code before using in production.
Low trust in AI for final story decisions; relies on human editorial judgment.
Sees AI outputs as starting points, not final answers.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive coding or data cleaning.
Believes human oversight is essential for nuanced storytelling and editorial decisions.
Advocates for hybrid workflows where AI accelerates, but humans validate, outputs.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for low-risk, data-heavy tasks (e.g., data normalization).
Finds high automation threatening in areas requiring ethical judgment or nuanced reporting.
Supports automation only when manual override and audit trails are available.

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of analytical and critical thinking skills.
Sees increased importance of prompt engineering and AI literacy.
Worries that basic data cleaning skills may become less valued due to automation.
Views storytelling and investigative skills as more resistant to AI disruption.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Learning to craft effective prompts for LLMs has become a new skill.
Responsible for validating and fact-checking AI-generated content.
Exploring basic scripting to integrate AI tools into existing workflows.
Keeping up with ethical considerations of AI use in journalism.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Moderately concerned about automation replacing repetitive data tasks.
Less worried about AI replacing core journalistic responsibilities.
Believes creative and investigative aspects of journalism are less susceptible to automation.
Monitors industry trends to anticipate future impacts.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Manually reviews all AI-generated code and analysis before publication.
No fully automated publishing pipeline; human oversight is required.
Organization encourages double-checking AI outputs for factual accuracy.
Peer review is standard practice for AI-assisted work.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team has become more open to AI experimentation in the past year.
No formal AI policy, but informal guidelines encourage responsible use.
Discussions on ethical AI use are increasing within the organization.
Company is monitoring developments before establishing official policies.

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will accelerate discovery and analysis in journalism.
Anticipates increased transparency and accountability through automated fact-checking.
Worries about potential for misinformation amplified by generative AI.
Sees potential for AI to democratize access to data-driven reporting.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Concerned about bias in AI-generated content and analysis.
Believes transparency in AI use is essential for journalistic integrity.
Advocates for clear guidelines and human oversight when using AI.
Supports ongoing education on ethical AI practices for journalists.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires AI tools that can better clean and standardize messy public datasets.
Would benefit from AI that can automatically flag data anomalies or outliers.
Wants AI writing assistants tailored for journalistic style and accuracy.
Seeks automated tools for verifying and citing sources in real time.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects routine data analysis and cleaning to become largely automated.
Believes focus will shift toward interpreting AI-generated insights and storytelling.
Predicts new roles in overseeing AI ethics and transparency in newsrooms.
Anticipates increased need for multidisciplinary collaboration with technologists.

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Establish clear ethical guidelines for AI use in reporting and publishing.
Provide formal training for journalists on AI tools and best practices.
Invest in developing proprietary AI tools tailored to newsroom needs.
Encourage cross-team collaboration to explore new AI use cases.
Regularly review and update policies as AI technology evolves.
